Topic-to-Article Linker - Woman’s Exponent

Birch

2026-06-17

Topic-to-Article Linker

This notebook runs STM topic modeling on whatever Woman’s Exponent volumes are placed in the input folder, then links each topic back to specific articles from the inventory so you can identify concrete evidentiary examples for each topic bucket. Just drop WEVol## folders into the input directory and run.

Caveat (K = 35 run): The visualizations in this notebook have not been fully run on the current K = 35 model. The virtual environment kept timing out before the plotting chunks completed, so some figures (and the saved stm_model_K35.rds / topic_linker_session_K35.RData artifacts) may be missing or stale. Treat the K = 35 outputs as provisional until a full, uninterrupted run completes.

1. Load Packages

library(ragg)
library(tm)
library(stm)
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stopwords)
library(DT)
library(textstem)    # For lemmatization
library(pheatmap)    # For heatmaps
library(scales)      # For percent formatting
library(wordcloud)
library(ggridges)    # For ridgeline plots
library(patchwork)   # For plot composition (|, /)
library(ggrepel)     # For labeled scatter points


set.seed(12345)
cat("Packages loaded.\n")

2. Set Paths

# ---- Scenario (corpus slice) ----------------------------------------------
# Pick which slice of the corpus to model. Each scenario writes to its own
# subfolder output_linked/<tag>/ so runs never collide.
#   "WEfull"      -> entire corpus (no year filter)
#   "WE1872-1891" -> years 1872-1891
#   "WE1892-1912" -> years 1892-1912
#   "auto"        -> run on WHATEVER volumes are physically in input_path and
#                    derive the tag from the observed year span. Drop volumes
#                    into input/, Run All, and it self-names the output folder.
scenario <- "WEfull"
.scenario_ranges <- list(
  "WEfull"      = c(-Inf, Inf),
  "WE1872-1891" = c(1872, 1891),
  "WE1892-1912" = c(1892, 1912)
)
scenario_years <- if (scenario %in% names(.scenario_ranges)) {
  .scenario_ranges[[scenario]]
} else {
  c(-Inf, Inf)   # "auto"/unknown: no year filter; corpus = whatever's in input
}

# Environment-aware roots. On Windows EVERYTHING runs from the local Rachel hard
# drive -- corpus, inventory, and output -- so OneDrive sync never touches the
# heavy I/O. The corpus reads from the local Topicmodel/input (the full WE canon,
# or whatever volumes you drop there for an "auto" run). The scenario subfolder is
# appended to output_root in section 5, once the observed year range is known.
if (.Platform$OS.type == "windows") {
  input_path     <- "C:/Users/birch/Rachel/GMU/Dissertation/textanalysis/Topicmodel/input/"
  output_root    <- "C:/Users/birch/Rachel/GMU/Dissertation/textanalysis/Claudepythonexperimentadvtext/output_linked"
  inventory_path <- "C:/Users/birch/Rachel/GMU/Dissertation/textanalysis/exponentorder/output/WE_article_inventory_vols1-41.xlsx"
} else {
  input_path     <- "/home/rbirch/rbirch/Topicmodel/input/"
  output_root    <- "/home/rbirch/rbirch/Topicmodel/output"
  inventory_path <- "/home/rbirch/rbirch/exponentorder/WE_article_inventory_vols1-41.xlsx"
}

# output_path is finalized in section 5 (the "auto" tag needs the observed years);
# point it at the root for now so any earlier write still lands somewhere sane.
output_path <- output_root
if (!dir.exists(output_root)) dir.create(output_root, recursive = TRUE)

cat("Scenario: ", scenario, " | year filter: ",
    if (all(is.finite(scenario_years))) paste(scenario_years, collapse = "-") else "none",
    "\n", sep = "")
cat("Input:      ", input_path, "\n")
cat("Output root:", output_root, "\n")
cat("Inventory:  ", inventory_path, "\n")
cat("OS:                  ", .Platform$OS.type, "\n")
cat("Default bitmap type: ", getOption("bitmapType"), "\n")
cat("Cairo available:     ", capabilities("cairo"), "\n")
cat("Default device:      "); print(getOption("device"))

3. Load Article Inventory

inventory <- read_excel(inventory_path, sheet = "Article Inventory")

cat("Inventory loaded:", nrow(inventory), "articles\n")
cat("Columns:", paste(names(inventory), collapse = ", "), "\n\n")

# Detect which volumes are in the input folder
input_vol_dirs <- list.dirs(input_path, recursive = FALSE, full.names = FALSE)
input_vol_nums <- as.integer(str_extract(input_vol_dirs, "\\d+"))
input_vol_nums <- sort(input_vol_nums[!is.na(input_vol_nums)])

cat("Volumes detected in input:", paste(input_vol_nums, collapse = ", "), "\n")

# Filter inventory to only the volumes present in input
inventory_subset <- inventory %>%
  filter(Volume %in% input_vol_nums)

cat("Matching inventory articles:", nrow(inventory_subset), "\n")

4. Import Article Files

all_files <- list.files(path = input_path, full.names = TRUE, recursive = TRUE)
file_paths <- all_files[grepl("\\.txt$", all_files, ignore.case = TRUE)]

cat("Total .txt files found:", length(file_paths), "\n\n")

# ---- Header-stripping helper ----
# Many article .txt files start with metadata headers that, if left in,
# pollute the corpus — "TITLE: X\nAUTHOR: Y\nDATE: Z" lines bleed in as
# regular words and the author's name ends up repeated 2-3x per file.
# Strip both formats: bracket-style `[Title: X]` and key:value lines.
strip_metadata_header <- function(raw) {
  # Strip BOM if present
  raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw, "^\\uFEFF")

  metadata_keywords <- c("title", "author", "date", "filename",
                         "year", "volume", "issue", "page", "type")
  keyword_alt <- paste(metadata_keywords, collapse = "|")

  prev <- ""
  while (raw != prev) {
    prev <- raw
    # Bracket-style header  e.g. "[Title: X]"
    raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw, "^\\[[^\\]]*\\]\\s*\\n?")
    # "Vol 26 Issue 23b" / "Issue 23" identifier on its own line
    raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw,
      "(?im)^(?:vol(?:ume)?\\s*\\d+[a-z]?\\b\\s*)?(?:issue\\s*\\d+[a-z]?\\b\\s*)?\\n")
    # Bare issue identifier like "23b" alone on the first line
    raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw, "(?i)^\\d+[a-z]?\\s*\\n")
    # "Keyword: value\n" line (the colon form)
    raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw,
      paste0("(?im)^(", keyword_alt, ")\\s*:[^\\n]*\\n"))
    # "Keyword\nValue\n" (label on its own line, value on next line)
    raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw,
      paste0("(?im)^(", keyword_alt, ")\\s*\\n[^\\n]+\\n"))
    # Leading blank lines
    raw <- stringr::str_remove(raw, "^\\s*\\n+")
  }
  raw
}

# Read all files, stripping metadata headers as we go
raw_texts <- tibble(
  filepath = file_paths,
  filename = basename(file_paths),
  text = map_chr(file_paths, ~strip_metadata_header(read_file(.x)))
)

cat("Read", nrow(raw_texts), "files.\n")

5. Extract Metadata & Join to Inventory

# Extract metadata from file path structure
raw_texts <- raw_texts %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse volume from WEVol## directory
    volume_dir = str_extract(filepath, "WEVol\\d+"),
    volume_num = as.integer(str_extract(volume_dir, "\\d+")),
    # Parse from filename: WE_Vol32_1903_Issue1_002_TITLE.txt
    year = as.integer(str_extract(filename, "(?<=Vol\\d{1,2}_)\\d{4}")),
    issue = str_extract(filename, "(?<=Issue)\\d+"),
    article_number = as.integer(str_extract(filename, "(?<=Issue\\d{1,2}_)\\d{3}")),
    doc_id = row_number(),
    word_count = str_count(text, "\\S+")
  )

# ---- Scenario year filter + output folder (see section 2 set-paths) ----
# Named scenarios slice the full corpus by year; "auto" keeps whatever volumes
# are physically in input_path. Once observed years are known we finalize the
# output subfolder (and, for "auto", its tag), so ALL output_path writes below
# land in output_linked/<tag>/.
if (all(is.finite(scenario_years))) {
  .before <- nrow(raw_texts)
  raw_texts <- raw_texts %>%
    filter(!is.na(year), year >= scenario_years[1], year <= scenario_years[2]) %>%
    mutate(doc_id = row_number())
  cat("Scenario '", scenario, "': kept ", nrow(raw_texts), " of ", .before,
      " docs in years [", scenario_years[1], "-", scenario_years[2], "]\n", sep = "")
}
scenario_tag <- if (scenario %in% names(.scenario_ranges)) {
  scenario
} else {
  sprintf("WE%d-%d", min(raw_texts$year, na.rm = TRUE), max(raw_texts$year, na.rm = TRUE))
}
output_path <- file.path(output_root, scenario_tag)
if (!dir.exists(output_path)) dir.create(output_path, recursive = TRUE)
cat("Scenario tag: ", scenario_tag, "  ->  ", output_path, "\n", sep = "")
cat("  (use this tag for the report's `scenario` and Python ADV_SCENARIO)\n")

# Join to inventory by filename
raw_texts <- raw_texts %>%
  left_join(
    inventory_subset %>% select(Filename, Title, Author, Article_Type, Volume, Month),
    by = c("filename" = "Filename")
  )

match_count <- sum(!is.na(raw_texts$Title))
cat("Matched to inventory:", match_count, "of", nrow(raw_texts), "files\n")
cat("Unmatched:", sum(is.na(raw_texts$Title)), "\n\n")

# Filter the corpus: keep all substantive content INCLUDING ads (so STM can
# form a dedicated ad/commerce topic), but exclude the genuinely non-content
# documents — TOC-style Front Matter pages and the subscription-rates Masthead.
# Section 5b below is now redundant (ads are not dropped here) but left as a
# safety net in case `Article_Type` is missing for some rows.
modeled_types <- c("Article", "Editorial", "Obituary", "Correspondence",
                   "Relief Society Report", "Board/Meeting", "Resolution",
                   "Poetry", "Advertisement")
raw_texts <- raw_texts %>%
  filter(is.na(Article_Type) | Article_Type %in% modeled_types)

cat("Modeled corpus:", nrow(raw_texts), "documents\n")
cat("  including ads:", sum(raw_texts$Article_Type == "Advertisement", na.rm = TRUE), "\n")
cat("Excluded as non-content (Front Matter / Masthead): see above\n\n")

# Show sample
raw_texts %>%
  filter(!is.na(Title)) %>%
  select(filename, Title, Author, Article_Type, Volume, year) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  print()

6. Text Cleaning

standard_stopwords <- stopwords::stopwords("en", source = "snowball")

custom_stopwords <- c(
  "the", "of", "and", "in", "to", "a", "is", "that", "it", "for",
  "as", "on", "with", "be", "was", "by", "are", "from", "or", "an",
  "at", "this", "which", "have", "has", "had", "but", "not", "can",
  "will", "been", "were", "their", "they", "we", "our", "would",
  "there", "what", "so", "if", "who", "than", "may", "also",
  "mrs", "mr", "miss", "dr", "ms",
  "january", "february", "march", "april", "may", "june",
  "july", "august", "september", "october", "november", "december",
  "vol", "page", "number", "volume", "board", "officer", "general",
  "thee", "thou", "whom", "no", "num", "pp", "pg",
  "issue", "nth", "th", "st", "nd", "rd",
  "womansexponent", "exponent",
    # Metadata-header words (belt-and-suspenders against header-stripping misses)
  "title", "author", "authors", "byline", "date", "filename",
  "year", "vol", "volumes", "page", "pages", "issn", "doi",
  # Author-placeholder words — bare "Unknown" / "Anonymous" tokens that
  # the extraction pipeline writes when the original byline is missing.
  # Without this, every author-less document carries a token "unknown"
  # which the model then clusters around.
  "unknown", "anonymous", "none",
  # Masthead / publication boilerplate
  "editor", "publisher", "publishe", "published", "publish",
  "assistant", "monthly", "salt", "lake", "city", "utah",
  "term", "copy", "year", "month", "cts", "cent",
  "subscription", "subscribe", "postage", "mail", "deliver",
  "advertise", "advertiser", "advertisement", "advertising",
  
  # Table of contents vocabulary
  "content", "contents", "frontmatter", "front", "matter",
  # Common structural words in programs and listings
  "program", "report", "reports", "committee", "meeting",
  "secretary", "sec", "secy", "asst", "pres", "treas",
  "treasurer", "president", "vice"
)

years_as_words <- as.character(unique(na.omit(raw_texts$year)))

# OCR fragment / junk word blacklist — common broken words from digitized text
ocr_junk_stopwords <- c(
  # Suffix-only fragments
  "ing", "tion", "ment", "ness", "ings", "tive", "ance", "ence",
  "ered", "ters", "bers", "dren", "howe", "ould", "ight", "ther",
  "ated", "ting", "ally", "ible", "able", "ious", "eous", "ical",
  "onal", "ship", "ward", "wise", "less",
  # Truncated word fragments from OCR
  "deliv", "presi", "inter", "encour", "terest", "testi",
  "soci", "coun", "chil", "pariel", "organiza", "ganized",
  "ference", "musser", "selion",
  # Structural boilerplate remnants
  "column", "columns", "chapter", "section", "article",
  "continued", "copyright", "printed"
)

all_stopwords <- unique(c(standard_stopwords, custom_stopwords,
                          ocr_junk_stopwords, years_as_words))

# Regex patterns to catch OCR fragments not in the explicit list
is_ocr_fragment <- function(w) {
  grepl("^[a-z]{1,3}(tion|ment|ence|ness|ings|tive|ance)$", w) |
  grepl("^(pre|suf|dis|un|re)[a-z]{0,2}$", w) |
  grepl("^[^a-z]+$", w)
}

# ============================================================
# OCR VARIANT REMEDIATION
# Folds known OCR misreads into canonical forms BEFORE lemmatization,
# since lemmatize_words() only normalizes recognized English words.
# Build/edit this list from the `ocr-variant-audit` chunk output.
# ============================================================
ocr_fixes <- c(
  # Suffrage family
  "\\bsuffage\\b"       = "suffrage",
  "\\bsuffeage\\b"      = "suffrage",
  "\\bsufferage\\b"     = "suffrage",
  "\\bsuffiage\\b"      = "suffrage",
  "\\bsuffra\\b"        = "suffrage",
  "\\bsffrage\\b"       = "suffrage",
  "\\bsnffrage\\b"      = "suffrage",
  "\\bsuffrag\\b"       = "suffrage",   # OCR fragment (missing 'e')
  "\\bsuffragea\\b"     = "suffrage",   # OCR variant
  "\\bsuffragean\\b"    = "suffrage",   # OCR variant
  "\\bsuffraged\\b"     = "suffrage",   # OCR (no English verb form)
  "\\bsuffagist\\b"     = "suffragist",
  # Note: "suffragans" (assistant bishops) is intentionally NOT folded — different word.
  # Retrenchment family
  "\\brefrenchment\\b"  = "retrenchment",
  "\\bretrechment\\b"   = "retrenchment",
  "\\bretrenchmeet\\b"  = "retrenchment",
  "\\bretrenchmet\\b"   = "retrenchment",
  "\\bretrnchment\\b"   = "retrenchment",
  # Kimball — OCR misreads only
  "\\bkimbal\\b"        = "kimball",
  "\\bkilmball\\b"      = "kimball",
  "\\bkimbalt\\b"       = "kimball",
  "\\bkinball\\b"       = "kimball",
  "\\bkmball\\b"        = "kimball",
  # Note: "brimhall" is a distinct Mormon surname — NOT folded.
  # Edmunds (Senator George F. Edmunds / Edmunds Act 1882)
  "\\bedends\\b"        = "edmunds",
  "\\bcoedmons\\b"      = "edmunds",
  "\\bedmnnds\\b"       = "edmunds",
  "\\bedmuuds\\b"       = "edmunds",
  "\\bedmundses\\b"     = "edmunds",
  "\\bedmundss\\b"      = "edmunds",
  "\\bedmond\\b"        = "edmunds",    # OCR variant per author judgment
  "\\bedmonds\\b"       = "edmunds",    # OCR variant per author judgment
  "\\bedmund\\b"        = "edmunds",    # OCR variant per author judgment
  # Legislature / constitution
  "\\blegilature\\b"    = "legislature",
  "\\blegislatnre\\b"   = "legislature",
  "\\bconititutional\\b" = "constitutional",
  "\\bconsititution\\b"  = "constitution",
  "\\bconsitution\\b"    = "constitution",
  "\\bconstitntion\\b"   = "constitution",
  "\\bconstittuion\\b"   = "constitution",
  "\\bconstituion\\b"    = "constitution",
  # Note: "constitutents" deliberately NOT folded — could be "constituents" (different word).
  # Prosecute / prosecution — federal legal action against polygamists.
  # CRITICAL: persecute/persecution are NOT folded. Those are different English words
  # carrying the Mormon counter-framing of federal action as religious oppression.
  # The contrast prosecute↔persecute is analytically essential and must be preserved.
  "\\bpresecution\\b"   = "prosecution",
  "\\bproecution\\b"    = "prosecution",
  "\\bprosecu\\b"       = "prosecute",
  "\\bprosecut\\b"      = "prosecute",
  # Note: "prosecutor" (the person) intentionally NOT folded.
  # Reynolds (Reynolds v. United States 1879)
  "\\brynolds\\b"       = "reynolds",
  # Polygamy family — collapse OCR errors AND grammatical variants into one bucket.
  # Trade-off: stronger topic signal but loses distinction between the practice
  # (polygamy), practitioners (polygamist), and adjectival forms (polygamic).
  # Re-separate if your analysis needs that grammatical precision.
  "\\blygamist\\b"      = "polygamy",   # OCR fragment of polygamist
  "\\blygamists\\b"     = "polygamy",   # OCR fragment of polygamists
  "\\bpolygamista\\b"   = "polygamy",   # OCR misread
  "\\bpolygamic\\b"     = "polygamy",   # adjective form
  "\\bpolygamical\\b"   = "polygamy",   # adjective form
  "\\bpolygamist\\b"    = "polygamy",   # noun, person
  "\\bpolygamists\\b"   = "polygamy"    # plural of polygamist
)

# ============================================================
# SURNAME PROTECTION
# Maps surnames to opaque tokens so lemmatization doesn't collapse them
# into common nouns (e.g., "wells" → "well", losing Emmeline B. Wells).
#
# Context-anchored: requires a known first name or title to precede the
# surname. This prevents tagging adjective/noun uses of "young", "snow",
# "wells" that share spelling with the surnames. Trade-off: bare-surname
# references (e.g., "Wells wrote...") will NOT be tagged; those fall back
# to ordinary lemmatization. In Woman's Exponent, titled references
# ("Sister Wells", "Mrs. Wells", "Emmeline B. Wells") dominate.
#
# Pattern structure: (first_name|title) [optional middle initials] surname
# After topic fitting, map these back to readable names for display.
# ============================================================
name_protect <- c(
  # Pattern: (first_name|title) [optional single-letter initials, 0-3 of them] surname
  # The character class [b-hj-z] excludes 'a' and 'i' to avoid matching them as initials.
  # Trade-off: middle names spelled out (e.g., "Mary Ann Smith") will NOT match.
  # Wells — Emmeline B. Wells (editor 1877–1914)
  "\\b(emmeline|mrs|sister|elder)(\\s+[b-hj-z]\\b){0,3}\\s+wells\\b" = "wellssurname",
  # Young — Brigham, Zina D. H., titles. Negative lookahead excludes the
  # YLMIA-style organizational compounds where "Young" is part of a collective
  # noun ("Young Ladies' Mutual Improvement Assoc.", "Young Men's Society")
  # that titles can incidentally precede.
  "\\b(brigham|zina|mrs|sister|brother|president|elder)(\\s+[b-hj-z]\\b){0,3}\\s+young\\b(?!\\s+(ladies|lady|men|man|women|woman|people|folks|children|girls|boys))" = "youngsurname",
  # Snow — Eliza R. Snow, Lorenzo Snow
  "\\b(eliza|lorenzo|mrs|sister|brother|president|elder)(\\s+[b-hj-z]\\b){0,3}\\s+snow\\b" = "snowsurname",
  # Smith — Joseph F./Joseph III/Bathsheba W./Hyrum/Emma/Mary
  "\\b(joseph|bathsheba|emma|mary|hyrum|mrs|sister|brother|president|elder)(\\s+[b-hj-z]\\b){0,3}\\s+smith\\b" = "smithsurname"
)

processed_texts <- raw_texts %>%
  mutate(
    text_clean = str_to_lower(text),
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "http\\S+"),
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "\\S+@\\S+"),
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "\\bvol\\.?\\s*\\d+\\b"),
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "\\bno\\.?\\s*\\d+\\b"),
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "\\bp\\.?\\s*\\d+\\b"),
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "\\b\\d+\\b"),
    # ICU regex note: [[:punct:]] alone misses math/other symbols (= < > + etc.)
    # that 19th-century OCR routinely produces as soft-hyphen / line-break
    # artifacts. The [:symbol:] class catches those.
    text_clean = str_remove_all(text_clean, "[[:punct:][:symbol:]]"),
    text_clean = str_replace_all(text_clean, ocr_fixes),
    text_clean = str_replace_all(text_clean, name_protect),
    text_clean = str_squish(text_clean),
    words = str_split(text_clean, "\\s+"),
    words_filtered = map(words, ~.x[!.x %in% all_stopwords]),
    words_filtered = map(words_filtered, ~.x[!grepl("^\\d+$", .x)]),
    words_filtered = map(words_filtered, ~.x[nchar(.x) >= 3 & !is_ocr_fragment(.x)]),
    # Lemmatize: women->woman, churches->church, etc.
    words_lemma = map(words_filtered, ~lemmatize_words(.x)),
    # Re-filter stopwords after lemmatization (some lemmas become stopwords)
    words_final = map(words_lemma, ~.x[!.x %in% all_stopwords & nchar(.x) >= 3 & !is_ocr_fragment(.x)]),
    text_final = map_chr(words_final, ~paste(.x, collapse = " ")),
    final_word_count = str_count(text_final, "\\S+")
  )

cat("Avg words before:", round(mean(raw_texts$word_count)), "\n")
cat("Avg words after:", round(mean(processed_texts$final_word_count)), "\n")

processed_texts <- processed_texts %>%
  filter(final_word_count >= 5)    # was 10 — lowered to preserve short poems
                                   # and brief notices that have substantive content

processed_texts <- processed_texts %>%
  mutate(
    unique_token_ratio = purrr::map_dbl(words_final, function(w) {
      if (length(w) < 3) return(1)
      length(unique(w)) / length(w)
    }),
    # Count of distinct post-stopword, post-lemmatization tokens.
    # This is what really matters for topic modeling: the number of
    # distinct "ideas" in a document, not the raw token count.
    n_unique_tokens = purrr::map_int(words_final, ~length(unique(.x)))
  )

n_low_density <- processed_texts %>%
  filter(unique_token_ratio < 0.45,
         !Article_Type %in% c("Poetry", "Poem")) %>%
  nrow()

processed_texts <- processed_texts %>%
  filter(unique_token_ratio >= 0.45 |
         Article_Type %in% c("Poetry", "Poem"))

cat("Dropped", n_low_density, "low-density docs (poems exempt)\n")

# ---- Minimum-unique-tokens filter ----
# Density catches WIDE-but-shallow repetition; this catches NARROW-and-shallow
# documents — correspondence stubs, header-only orphans, OCR fragments where
# only the title/date words survived. A real article (even a brief death
# notice like "Constance Fenimore Woolson novelist die jan venice grandniece
# james fenimore cooper") has 8-10+ distinct content tokens. A correspondence
# stub like "letter arizona zenos nov 24th" has 4-5.
#
# Threshold of 7 unique tokens lands in the gap between these.
# POEMS ARE EXEMPT — same logic as the density filter.
n_too_thin <- processed_texts %>%
  filter(n_unique_tokens < 7,
         !Article_Type %in% c("Poetry", "Poem")) %>%
  nrow()

processed_texts <- processed_texts %>%
  filter(n_unique_tokens >= 7 |
         Article_Type %in% c("Poetry", "Poem"))

cat("Dropped", n_too_thin, "thin-content docs (< 7 unique tokens, poems exempt)\n")
cat("Documents after filtering:", nrow(processed_texts), "\n\n")

# --- Deduplicate near-identical documents (token-bag Jaccard clustering) ---
# Recurring articles (esp. railroad ads) appear in many issues as near-identical
# copies with OCR drift ("chicago" vs "chicaga" vs "chichaga"). A literal
# substring fingerprint can't catch these — every copy's first 200 characters
# differ by a few letters. Instead, compare the SET of unique post-lemma tokens
# per document with Jaccard similarity, then collapse high-similarity clusters
# (igraph connected components) down to one representative each.
cat("Deduplicating near-identical documents (Jaccard clustering)...\n")
n_before_dedup <- nrow(processed_texts)

jaccard_threshold <- 0.80   # 0.8 = collapse docs sharing 80%+ of unique vocab
fingerprint_min_len <- 5    # skip ultra-short docs to avoid spurious matches

token_sets <- lapply(processed_texts$words_final, function(w) sort(unique(w)))
set_sizes  <- vapply(token_sets, length, integer(1))

# Build sparse edge list with a length-ratio prefilter so we don't do a full
# n^2 sweep on the full corpus. Two docs can only clear `jaccard_threshold`
# if their size ratio also does — that lets us skip most pairs in O(1).
n_docs <- length(token_sets)
edges <- vector("list", 0)
ord <- order(set_sizes)  # process short-to-long so we can early-exit inner loop

for (a in seq_len(n_docs - 1)) {
  i <- ord[a]
  si <- set_sizes[i]
  if (si < fingerprint_min_len) next
  # Inner loop only needs to consider docs whose size is within the ratio band.
  for (b in (a + 1):n_docs) {
    j <- ord[b]
    sj <- set_sizes[j]
    if (sj < fingerprint_min_len) next
    if (si / sj < jaccard_threshold) break   # sorted by size, so all later j fail too
    inter <- length(intersect(token_sets[[i]], token_sets[[j]]))
    union <- si + sj - inter
    if (union == 0) next
    if (inter / union >= jaccard_threshold) {
      edges[[length(edges) + 1]] <- c(i, j)
    }
  }
}

# Connected components via Union-Find (no external graph library needed).
# Each near-duplicate edge merges two docs; final clusters are connected sets.
if (length(edges) > 0) {
  uf <- new.env(parent = emptyenv())
  uf$parent <- seq_len(n_docs)

  uf_find <- function(x) {
    # Walk to root, then compress the path so future lookups are O(1).
    root <- x
    while (uf$parent[root] != root) root <- uf$parent[root]
    while (uf$parent[x] != root) {
      nxt <- uf$parent[x]
      uf$parent[x] <- root
      x <- nxt
    }
    root
  }

  uf_union <- function(a, b) {
    ra <- uf_find(a); rb <- uf_find(b)
    if (ra != rb) uf$parent[ra] <- rb
  }

  for (e in edges) uf_union(e[1], e[2])

  cluster_id <- vapply(seq_len(n_docs), uf_find, integer(1))
} else {
  cluster_id <- seq_len(n_docs)   # every doc its own cluster
}

processed_texts <- processed_texts %>%
  mutate(.dup_cluster = cluster_id) %>%
  group_by(.dup_cluster) %>%
  slice_max(final_word_count, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%  # keep most complete copy
  ungroup() %>%
  select(-.dup_cluster) %>%
  mutate(doc_id = row_number())

cat("  Near-duplicate pairs found:", length(edges), "\n")
cat("  Removed", n_before_dedup - nrow(processed_texts), "near-duplicate documents\n")
cat("  Corpus after dedup:", nrow(processed_texts), "documents\n")
# ============================================================
# REMEDIATION ACCOUNTING (for dissertation methodology appendix)
# Documents how many raw-text tokens were folded by ocr_fixes and
# how many surnames were tagged by name_protect. Produces a paper
# trail of the preprocessing decisions that altered vocabulary.
# Saves a CSV for inclusion in the methods chapter.
# ============================================================

count_token_occurrences <- function(term, texts) {
  # Case-insensitive word-boundary count over the raw corpus
  pattern <- paste0("\\b", tolower(term), "\\b")
  sum(stringr::str_count(tolower(texts), pattern))
}

# ---- OCR variant folding ----
ocr_lookup <- tibble::tibble(
  pattern   = names(ocr_fixes),
  canonical = unname(ocr_fixes)
) %>%
  mutate(variant = stringr::str_remove_all(pattern, "\\\\b")) %>%
  group_by(canonical) %>%
  summarise(variants = list(variant), .groups = "drop")

remediation_table <- ocr_lookup %>%
  mutate(
    canonical_count_raw  = purrr::map_dbl(canonical, count_token_occurrences,
                                          texts = raw_texts$text),
    variant_count_folded = purrr::map_dbl(variants, function(vs) {
      sum(purrr::map_dbl(vs, count_token_occurrences, texts = raw_texts$text))
    }),
    post_fold_count      = canonical_count_raw + variant_count_folded,
    pct_recovered        = round(100 * variant_count_folded /
                                 pmax(canonical_count_raw, 1), 1),
    n_variants_folded    = lengths(variants),
    variants_list        = purrr::map_chr(variants, ~paste(.x, collapse = ", "))
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(variant_count_folded)) %>%
  select(canonical, canonical_count_raw, variant_count_folded,
         post_fold_count, pct_recovered, n_variants_folded, variants_list)

cat("=== OCR Remediation Accounting ===\n")
cat("Variant folding (raw text → canonical form):\n\n")
print(remediation_table %>% select(-variants_list), n = Inf, width = Inf)

cat("\nTotals across all canonical terms:\n")
cat("  Canonical occurrences (pre-fold):  ", sum(remediation_table$canonical_count_raw), "\n")
cat("  Variant tokens folded in:          ", sum(remediation_table$variant_count_folded), "\n")
cat("  Canonical occurrences (post-fold): ", sum(remediation_table$post_fold_count), "\n")
cat("  Net recovery rate:                 ",
    round(100 * sum(remediation_table$variant_count_folded) /
          pmax(sum(remediation_table$canonical_count_raw), 1), 1), "%\n")

# ---- Surname protection (context-anchored counting) ----
# Counts both:
#   (a) tokens_tagged    — bare-surname occurrences captured by the
#                          context-anchored pattern (real name references)
#   (b) bare_count_raw   — all bare-surname occurrences in raw text
#                          (includes adjective/noun uses like "young woman")
#   (c) bare_unprotected — bare_count_raw - tokens_tagged
#                          (uses that fall back to ordinary lemmatization,
#                           mostly common-word meanings)
name_table <- tibble::tibble(
  pattern = names(name_protect),
  opaque  = unname(name_protect)
) %>%
  mutate(
    surname        = stringr::str_remove(opaque, "surname$"),
    tokens_tagged  = purrr::map_dbl(pattern, function(p) {
      sum(stringr::str_count(tolower(raw_texts$text), p))
    }),
    bare_count_raw   = purrr::map_dbl(surname, count_token_occurrences,
                                      texts = raw_texts$text),
    bare_unprotected = bare_count_raw - tokens_tagged,
    pct_protected    = round(100 * tokens_tagged /
                             pmax(bare_count_raw, 1), 1)
  ) %>%
  select(surname, opaque_token = opaque,
         tokens_tagged, bare_count_raw, bare_unprotected, pct_protected) %>%
  arrange(desc(tokens_tagged))

cat("\n=== Surname Protection Accounting ===\n")
cat("Context-anchored tagging — only first-name/title + surname patterns are protected.\n")
cat("'bare_unprotected' is the count of common-word uses that fall back to lemmatization.\n\n")
print(name_table, n = Inf, width = Inf)

# ---- Diagnostic: show sample tagged contexts ----
# Use this to spot false positives in the regex. If samples look like
# "sister young ladies", "president young men" — the regex needs more
# negative lookaheads. If samples look like "brigham young", "sister
# emmeline wells", "zina d young" — the regex is doing its job.
cat("\n=== Tagged Context Samples (sanity check the regex) ===\n")
set.seed(2026)  # reproducible samples
all_text <- tolower(paste(raw_texts$text, collapse = " | "))
for (i in seq_along(name_protect)) {
  pattern <- names(name_protect)[i]
  opaque  <- unname(name_protect)[i]

  # Capture pattern + 2 surrounding words on each side for context
  ctx_pattern <- paste0("(?:\\S+\\s+){0,2}", pattern, "(?:\\s+\\S+){0,2}")
  matches <- stringr::str_extract_all(all_text, ctx_pattern)[[1]]

  cat("\n--- ", opaque, " (", length(matches), " matches; showing up to 8) ---\n", sep = "")
  if (length(matches) > 0) {
    samples <- sample(matches, min(8, length(matches)))
    cat(paste0("  ", samples, collapse = "\n"), "\n", sep = "")
  } else {
    cat("  (no matches)\n")
  }
}

# ---- Save for methodology appendix ----
write_csv(remediation_table,
          file.path(output_path, "remediation_accounting_ocr.csv"))
write_csv(name_table,
          file.path(output_path, "remediation_accounting_names.csv"))
cat("\nSaved to:\n")
cat("  ", file.path(output_path, "remediation_accounting_ocr.csv"), "\n")
cat("  ", file.path(output_path, "remediation_accounting_names.csv"), "\n")

7. Prepare & Fit STM

# Fill NA Article_Type so prevalence covariates don't create row mismatches
processed_texts <- processed_texts %>%
  mutate(Article_Type = ifelse(is.na(Article_Type), "Unknown", Article_Type))

processed <- textProcessor(
  documents = processed_texts$text_final,
  metadata = processed_texts %>%
    select(doc_id, filename, volume_num, year, issue, Title, Author, Article_Type),
  lowercase = FALSE,
  removestopwords = FALSE,
  removenumbers = TRUE,
  removepunctuation = FALSE,
  stem = FALSE,
  wordLengths = c(3, Inf),
  sparselevel = 1,
  language = "en",
  verbose = TRUE
)

out <- prepDocuments(
  documents = processed$documents,
  vocab = processed$vocab,
  meta = processed$meta,
  lower.thresh = 3,   # keep singletons (rare legal terms like Edmunds-Tucker, specific case names)
  upper.thresh = Inf
)

docs <- out$documents
vocab <- out$vocab
meta <- out$meta

# Store the texts that actually made it into the model (for findThoughts later)
modeled_texts <- processed_texts$text_final[match(meta$doc_id, processed_texts$doc_id)]

cat("\nCorpus ready:", length(docs), "documents,", length(vocab), "vocab terms\n")
cat("  (dropped", nrow(processed_texts) - length(docs), "documents during preprocessing)\n")
# ============================================================
# OCR VARIANT AUDIT
# Scans final vocab for likely OCR misreads of historically important
# terms using approximate string matching (Levenshtein distance ≤ 2).
#
# Use results to build a str_replace_all() remediation table that runs
# BEFORE lemmatization. Lemmatization can't help — it only normalizes
# morphological variants of real words, not OCR errors.
# ============================================================

# Terms central to the suffrage / Edmunds Act / Mormon women's discourse.
# Edit this list to match what matters for your analysis.
audit_terms <- c(
  # Suffrage discourse
  "suffrage", "enfranchise", "franchise", "ballot", "legislature",
  "constitution", "amendment",
  # Polygamy / federal prosecution discourse
  "polygamy", "polygamist", "plural", "marriage", "cohabitation",
  "edmunds", "tucker", "reynolds", "prosecute", "prosecution",
  "raid", "indictment", "convict", "underground",
  # Mormon women's organizing
  "relief", "society", "primary", "retrenchment", "kimball", "wells",
  # Protected surname tokens — confirm name_protect worked.
  # These should show in_vocab = TRUE with 0 variants.
  "wellssurname", "youngsurname", "snowsurname", "smithsurname"
)

cat("=== OCR Variant Audit ===\n")
cat("Scanning vocab (n =", length(vocab), ") for variants within edit distance 2\n\n")

audit_results <- purrr::map_dfr(audit_terms, function(term) {
  # Length-proportional distance, capped at 2:
  # 4 chars → 1 edit | 5-8 chars → 2 | 9+ chars → 2 (capped)
  max_dist <- min(2L, ceiling(0.25 * nchar(term)))
  near <- agrep(term, vocab, max.distance = list(all = max_dist), value = TRUE)
  # Drop concatenation false positives — variant must be within ±2 chars of canonical
  near <- near[abs(nchar(near) - nchar(term)) <= 2]
  variants <- setdiff(near, term)
  tibble::tibble(
    canonical  = term,
    in_vocab   = term %in% vocab,
    n_variants = length(variants),
    variants   = if (length(variants) > 0) paste(variants, collapse = ", ") else "(none)"
  )
})

# Show terms that are missing OR have OCR variants worth folding
audit_results %>%
  filter(!in_vocab | n_variants > 0) %>%
  arrange(desc(n_variants), canonical) %>%
  print(n = Inf, width = Inf)

missing_terms <- audit_results %>% filter(!in_vocab)        %>% pull(canonical)
high_variant  <- audit_results %>% filter(n_variants >= 2) %>% pull(canonical)

cat("\n--- Summary ---\n")
if (length(missing_terms) > 0) {
  cat("Missing from vocab entirely (check OCR or preprocessing):\n  ",
      paste(missing_terms, collapse = ", "), "\n")
} else {
  cat("All audited terms present in vocab.\n")
}
if (length(high_variant) > 0) {
  cat("\nTerms with >=2 variants (highest payoff to fold):\n  ",
      paste(high_variant, collapse = ", "), "\n")
}

cat("\n--- Remediation pattern ---\n")
cat("Build a named vector and apply BEFORE lemmatization (around line 215):\n")
cat('  ocr_fixes <- c(\n')
cat('    "\\\\bfuffrage\\\\b"  = "suffrage",\n')
cat('    "\\\\bsuffrace\\\\b"  = "suffrage",\n')
cat('    "\\\\bedmund\\\\b"    = "edmunds"\n')
cat('  )\n')
cat('  text_clean <- str_replace_all(text_clean, ocr_fixes)\n')
# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC: searchK - tests multiple K values
# Shows held-out likelihood, residuals, semantic coherence,
# and lower bound to help justify your choice of K.
# Set eval=FALSE after running once to skip on re-runs.
# ============================================================
cat("Running searchK diagnostic (this may take 10-30 minutes)...\n")

K_candidates <- c(20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50)

searchK_results <- searchK(
  documents = docs,
  vocab = vocab,
  K = K_candidates,
  data = meta,
  prevalence = ~ s(year),
  init.type = "Spectral",
  seed = 12345,
  verbose = FALSE
)

# Render searchK diagnostic via ragg explicitly
ragg::agg_png(
  filename = file.path(output_path, "searchK_diagnostic.png"),
  width = 10, height = 8, units = "in", res = 200
)
plot(searchK_results)
dev.off()

# Also embed in notebook output
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(output_path, "searchK_diagnostic.png"))

# Save for reference
saveRDS(searchK_results, file.path(output_path, "searchK_results.rds"))
cat("searchK results saved. Use the plots to pick K.\n")
cat("Look for: high held-out likelihood, high semantic coherence,\n")
cat("low residuals, and a balance point on exclusivity vs coherence.\n")
# ============================================================
# Detailed 5-panel diagnostic: 4 metric line plots +
# coherence/exclusivity scatter labeled by K (upper-right = best).
# Set eval=TRUE to render alongside the faceted view.
# ============================================================
if (exists("searchK_results")) {

  sk_plot <- data.frame(
    K        = unlist(searchK_results$results$K),
    heldout  = unlist(searchK_results$results$heldout),
    residual = unlist(searchK_results$results$residual),
    semcoh   = unlist(searchK_results$results$semcoh),
    exclus   = unlist(searchK_results$results$exclus)
  )

  corpus_col <- "#CD661D"

  shared_scales <- list(
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = K_candidates),
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11),
    theme(legend.position = "none", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
  )

  p1 <- ggplot(sk_plot, aes(x = K, y = heldout)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, color = corpus_col) +
    geom_point(size = 3, color = corpus_col) +
    labs(title = "Held-Out Likelihood", subtitle = "Higher is better",
         x = "K", y = "Held-Out Likelihood") + shared_scales

  p2 <- ggplot(sk_plot, aes(x = K, y = residual)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, color = corpus_col) +
    geom_point(size = 3, color = corpus_col) +
    labs(title = "Residuals", subtitle = "Lower is better",
         x = "K", y = "Residuals") + shared_scales

  p3 <- ggplot(sk_plot, aes(x = K, y = semcoh)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, color = corpus_col) +
    geom_point(size = 3, color = corpus_col) +
    labs(title = "Semantic Coherence",
         subtitle = "Higher is better (trades off with exclusivity)",
         x = "K", y = "Semantic Coherence") + shared_scales

  p4 <- ggplot(sk_plot, aes(x = K, y = exclus)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, color = corpus_col) +
    geom_point(size = 3, color = corpus_col) +
    labs(title = "Exclusivity",
         subtitle = "Higher is better (trades off with coherence)",
         x = "K", y = "Exclusivity") + shared_scales

  p5 <- ggplot(sk_plot, aes(x = semcoh, y = exclus)) +
    geom_point(size = 4, alpha = 0.8, color = corpus_col) +
    geom_text(aes(label = K), size = 3.5, vjust = -1) +
    labs(title = "Coherence vs Exclusivity Tradeoff",
         subtitle = "Numbers = K value; upper-right = best quality",
         x = "Semantic Coherence", y = "Exclusivity") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

    combined_diagnostic <- (p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) / p5 +
    plot_annotation(
      title = "searchK Diagnostic: Woman's Exponent (1872–1892)",
      subtitle = "Each panel shows one quality metric across candidate K values",
      theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16))
    )

  # Save via ragg (bypasses any device issues)
  ragg::agg_png(
    filename = file.path(output_path, "searchK_detail_diagnostic.png"),
    width = 12, height = 10, units = "in", res = 200
  )
  print(combined_diagnostic)
  dev.off()

  # Also embed in the rendered notebook
  knitr::include_graphics(
    file.path(output_path, "searchK_detail_diagnostic.png")
  )
}
# ============================================================
# PARETO FRONTIER + HELD-OUT PLATEAU
# Range-independent K selection. Replaces the gap-minimization
# "sweet spot" which depends on the searchK range you chose.
#
# A K is Pareto-optimal if no other tested K has BOTH higher
# semantic coherence AND higher-or-equal exclusivity (or v.v.).
# Dominated K's are drawn faded — you'd never pick one over a
# frontier K. The held-out likelihood panel shows the plateau:
# smallest Pareto-optimal K on the plateau = parsimonious pick.
# ============================================================
if (exists("searchK_results")) {
  sk_data <- data.frame(
    K       = unlist(searchK_results$results$K),
    semcoh  = unlist(searchK_results$results$semcoh),
    exclus  = unlist(searchK_results$results$exclus),
    heldout = unlist(searchK_results$results$heldout)
  )
  # ----- Pareto status -----
  # Higher is better for both metrics. A point is dominated if some
  # other point beats it on one metric and ties-or-beats on the other.
  is_pareto <- function(coh, exc) {
    vapply(seq_along(coh), function(i) {
      !any( (coh >  coh[i] & exc >= exc[i]) |
            (coh >= coh[i] & exc >  exc[i]) )
    }, logical(1))
  }
  sk_data$pareto <- is_pareto(sk_data$semcoh, sk_data$exclus)
  frontier <- sk_data %>%
    filter(pareto) %>%
    arrange(semcoh)   # sort along the frontier curve
  cat("=== K Selection Diagnostic ===\n")
  cat("Pareto-optimal K values:", paste(sort(frontier$K), collapse = ", "), "\n")
  cat("Dominated K values:    ",
      paste(sort(sk_data$K[!sk_data$pareto]), collapse = ", "),
      ifelse(all(sk_data$pareto), "(none)", ""), "\n\n")
  corpus_col <- "#CD661D"
  # ----- Plot 1: Pareto frontier scatter -----
  p_pareto <- ggplot(sk_data, aes(x = semcoh, y = exclus)) +
    geom_line(data = frontier,
              color = corpus_col, linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_point(aes(color = pareto, shape = pareto, size = pareto),
               alpha = 0.9) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = paste0("K=", K), color = pareto),
                    size = 4, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE,
                    box.padding = 0.6, point.padding = 0.4,
                    seed = 12345) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("TRUE" = corpus_col, "FALSE" = "grey60"),
                       labels = c("TRUE" = "Pareto-optimal",
                                  "FALSE" = "Dominated")) +
    scale_shape_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 19, "FALSE" = 1),
                       labels = c("TRUE" = "Pareto-optimal",
                                  "FALSE" = "Dominated")) +
    scale_size_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 5, "FALSE" = 3),
                      labels = c("TRUE" = "Pareto-optimal",
                                 "FALSE" = "Dominated")) +
    labs(title = "Pareto Frontier — Coherence vs Exclusivity",
         subtitle = paste0("Filled = Pareto-optimal. Hollow = dominated ",
                           "(some other K beats it on both metrics)."),
         x = "Semantic Coherence (higher = better)",
         y = "Exclusivity (higher = better)",
         color = NULL, shape = NULL, size = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom",
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
          plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))
  # ----- Plot 2: Held-out likelihood with Pareto K's highlighted -----
  # The 'knee' is where the line transitions from rising to flat.
  # Smallest Pareto-optimal K past the knee = parsimonious choice.
  p_heldout <- ggplot(sk_data, aes(x = K, y = heldout)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.0, color = "grey50") +
    geom_point(aes(color = pareto, size = pareto), alpha = 0.9) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = paste0("K=", K), color = pareto),
                    size = 3.8, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE,
                    box.padding = 0.5, seed = 12345) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("TRUE" = corpus_col, "FALSE" = "grey60")) +
    scale_size_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 5, "FALSE" = 3)) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = K_candidates) +
    labs(title = "Held-Out Likelihood — Find the Plateau",
         subtitle = paste0("Orange = Pareto-optimal K. The 'knee' (slope ",
                           "flattens) is where adding K stops paying off."),
         x = "Number of Topics (K)",
         y = "Held-Out Likelihood (higher = better)") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "none",
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
          plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))
  # ----- Combined output -----
  (p_pareto / p_heldout) +
    plot_annotation(
      title    = "K Selection: Pareto Frontier + Held-Out Plateau",
      subtitle = paste0("Pick the smallest Pareto-optimal K that sits on ",
                        "the held-out plateau. Then INSPECT the topics."),
      theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16))
    )
 ragg::agg_png(
    filename = file.path(output_path, "K Selection: Pareto Frontier + Held-Out Plateau.png"),
    width = 12, height = 10, units = "in", res = 200
  )
  print(p_pareto / p_heldout)
  dev.off()

  # Also embed in the rendered notebook
  knitr::include_graphics(
    file.path(output_path, "K Selection: Pareto Frontier + Held-Out Plateau.png")
  )
}
# ============================================================
# SET NUMBER OF TOPICS HERE
# After running searchK above, pick the K that balances
# coherence and exclusivity. Or just set it manually.
# ============================================================
# chosen_K accepts either an integer (pins K) or "auto" (picks the balanced
# coherence/exclusivity point from the searchK run above). "auto" needs the
# searchK chunk to have produced searchK_results this session, or a saved
# searchK_results.rds in this scenario's output folder.
chosen_K <- 35   # or "auto"

if (identical(chosen_K, "auto")) {
  if (!exists("searchK_results")) {
    .skf <- file.path(output_path, "searchK_results.rds")
    if (file.exists(.skf)) searchK_results <- readRDS(.skf)
  }
  if (!exists("searchK_results")) {
    stop("chosen_K = 'auto' needs searchK results. Run the searchK chunk first ",
         "(or set chosen_K to an integer).")
  }
  .sk <- data.frame(
    K      = unlist(searchK_results$results$K),
    semcoh = unlist(searchK_results$results$semcoh),
    exclus = unlist(searchK_results$results$exclus)
  )
  # Rescale each metric to 0-1 and pick K maximizing their average — the
  # upper-right of the coherence/exclusivity frontier.
  .rescale <- function(x) if (diff(range(x)) == 0) rep(1, length(x)) else (x - min(x)) / diff(range(x))
  .sk$score <- (.rescale(.sk$semcoh) + .rescale(.sk$exclus)) / 2
  chosen_K <- .sk$K[which.max(.sk$score)]
  cat("Auto-selected K =", chosen_K, "(balanced coherence/exclusivity)\n")
} else {
  cat("K =", chosen_K, "\n")
}
chosen_K <- as.integer(chosen_K)
# Using prevalence covariates: the model learns how topic prevalence
# varies by year and article type during fitting, not just post-hoc.
stm_model <- stm(
  documents = docs,
  vocab = vocab,
  K = chosen_K,
  prevalence = ~ s(year) + Article_Type,
  data = meta,
  max.em.its = 75,
  init.type = "Spectral",
  seed = 12345,
  verbose = FALSE
)

saveRDS(stm_model, file.path(output_path, paste0("stm_model_K", chosen_K, ".rds")))
cat("Model saved.\n")

8. Examine Topics

all_top_words <- labelTopics(stm_model, n = 15)

# ---- Reverse-map protected surname tokens for display ----
# Mirrors `name_protect` from the preprocessing chunk.
# Edit this whenever name_protect changes.
surname_display <- c(
  "wellssurname" = "Wells",
  "youngsurname" = "Young",
  "snowsurname"  = "Snow",
  "smithsurname" = "Smith"
)
for (slot in intersect(names(all_top_words), c("prob", "frex", "lift", "score"))) {
  all_top_words[[slot]][] <- str_replace_all(all_top_words[[slot]], surname_display)
}

cat("Top words per topic:\n\n")
for (i in 1:chosen_K) {
  cat("Topic", i, "\n")
  cat("  FREX:", paste(all_top_words$frex[i, 1:10], collapse = ", "), "\n")
  cat("  PROB:", paste(all_top_words$prob[i, 1:10], collapse = ", "), "\n\n")
}

8b. Auto-Generate Topic Labels

Keyword rules try to assign descriptive labels based on FREX/PROB words. Topics that don’t match any rule get a label from their top 3 FREX words. You can add rules or override labels in the next section.

cat(rep("=", 70), "\n", sep = "")
cat("AUTO-GENERATING LABELS\n")
cat(rep("=", 70), "\n\n", sep = "")

create_specific_label <- function(topic_num, frex_words, prob_words) {
  frex_text <- tolower(paste(frex_words, collapse = " "))
  prob_text <- tolower(paste(prob_words, collapse = " "))
  all_words <- paste(frex_text, prob_text)

  # ---- RELIGION & DOCTRINE ----
  if (grepl("kingdom.*gospel|baptism.*eternal|salvation|atonement", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Gospel Doctrine & Spiritual Kingdom"
  } else if (grepl("passover|paschal|feast|tabernacle", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Biblical Symbolism & Ancient Rituals"
  } else if (grepl("abraham.*sarai|biblical|moses|israel", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Biblical Narratives"
  } else if (grepl("prayer|faith.*hope|testimony|spirit.*holy", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Faith & Spiritual Life"
  } else if (grepl("lesson|scripture|doctrine|commandment", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Religious Lessons & Scripture Study"
  } else if (grepl("temple|ordinance|endowment|sealing", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Temple & Sacred Ordinances"
  } else if (grepl("missionary|mission.*field|convert|preach", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Missionary Work & Conversion"

  # ---- RELIEF SOCIETY & CHURCH ORG ----
  } else if (grepl("sisters.*presidency|organization.*grain|relief.*society.*leader", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Relief Society Leadership & Organization"
  } else if (grepl("stake.*ward|attendance.*conference|quarterly", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Stake & Ward Relief Society Reports"
  } else if (grepl("empey|mclelland|board.*member|general.*board", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Board Member Directory"
  } else if (grepl("conference|general.*conference|session|sustain", all_words)) {
    label <- "Church Conference Proceedings"

  # ---- WOMEN'S RIGHTS & POLITICS ----
  } else if (grepl("suffrage.*vote|legislature|franchise|ballot", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Women's Suffrage & Political Rights"
  } else if (grepl("council.*international|convention|delegate", frex_text)) {
    label <- "International Women's Councils"
  } else if (grepl("equal.*right|petition|protest|reform", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Women's Reform & Activism"

  # ---- DOMESTICITY & HOME ----
  } else if (grepl("recipe|tbsp|boiling|oven|flour|bake", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Recipes & Cooking Instructions"
  } else if (grepl("housework|housekeeping|budget|thrift", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Household Management & Budgeting"
  } else if (grepl("child|mother.*rear|parent|infant|nurture", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Motherhood & Child-Rearing"
  } else if (grepl("garden|plant.*seed|flower|cultivation", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Home Gardening & Agriculture"
  } else if (grepl("sew|pattern|dress|fabric|garment|fashion", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Sewing, Fashion & Handwork"

  # ---- EDUCATION & PROFESSIONALIZATION ----
  } else if (grepl("nurse.*hospital|nursing.*training|medical", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Nursing Training & Hospital Work"
  } else if (grepl("school|teacher|student|education|university|class", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Education & Schools"
  } else if (grepl("diet.*protein|nutrition|malnutrition|vitamin", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Nutrition & Dietary Science"
  } else if (grepl("radium.*curie|pasteur.*science|scientist|discovery", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Scientific Biography & Discovery"

  # ---- HISTORY & PIONEER ----
  } else if (grepl("emigration.*journey|wagon.*pioneer|handcart|plains", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Pioneer Emigration & Journey Stories"
  } else if (grepl("pioneer|settlement|early.*days|frontier", all_words)) {
    label <- "Pioneer Heritage & Settlement"

  # ---- LITERATURE & ARTS ----
  } else if (grepl("irving|tale|novel|literary|author", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Literature & Book Reviews"
  } else if (grepl("jerry|walter|jimmy|agnes|margaret", frex_text) && grepl("said|little|came", prob_text)) {
    label <- "Serialized Fiction & Stories"
  } else if (grepl("poem|verse|stanza|rhyme|lyric", all_words)) {
    label <- "Poetry & Verse"
  } else if (grepl("tableau.*song|music.*hymn|choir|sing", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Musical Programs & Performances"

  # ---- WAR & CURRENT EVENTS ----
  } else if (grepl("entente.*allies|war.*troops|soldier|military|battle", frex_text)) {
    label <- "War Commentary & News"
  } else if (grepl("europe|nation|government|political|congress", frex_text)) {
    label <- "National & International Affairs"

  # ---- COMMERCE & ADS ----
  } else if (grepl("merino.*cotton|bleached.*gauze|advertis|price|store", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Advertisements & Commerce"
  } else if (grepl("^\\d+.*\\d+.*\\d+", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Magazine Index & Page Numbers"

  # ---- HEALTH ----
  } else if (grepl("health|disease|sanit|hygiene|cure|sick", all_words)) {
    label <- "Health & Sanitation"

  # ---- OBITUARY & BIOGRAPHY ----
  } else if (grepl("death|funeral|deceased|mourn|tribute", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Obituaries & Tributes"
  } else if (grepl("life.*born|biography|memoir|career|accomplish", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Biographical Sketches"

  # ---- CORRESPONDENCE ----
  } else if (grepl("letter|editor|dear|correspond|write", frex_text)) {
    label <- "Correspondence & Letters"

  # ---- FALLBACK: use top 3 FREX words ----
  } else {
    keywords <- paste(tools::toTitleCase(frex_words[1:3]), collapse = " / ")
    label <- keywords
  }

  return(label)
}

topic_labels <- sapply(1:chosen_K, function(i) {
  frex_words <- all_top_words$frex[i, 1:10]
  prob_words <- all_top_words$prob[i, 1:10]
  create_specific_label(i, frex_words, prob_words)
})

cat("Auto-generated labels:\n")
cat(rep("=", 70), "\n\n", sep = "")

for (i in 1:chosen_K) {
  cat("Topic", i, ":", topic_labels[i], "\n")
  cat("  FREX:", paste(all_top_words$frex[i, 1:7], collapse = ", "), "\n")
  cat("  PROB:", paste(all_top_words$prob[i, 1:7], collapse = ", "), "\n\n")
}

# Export auto-labels for reference
frex_for_export <- apply(all_top_words$frex, 1, function(x) paste(x, collapse = ", "))
prob_for_export <- apply(all_top_words$prob, 1, function(x) paste(x, collapse = ", "))

label_export <- tibble(
  topic_num = 1:chosen_K,
  auto_label = topic_labels,
  frex_words = frex_for_export,
  prob_words = prob_for_export,
  manual_label = ""
)

labels_file <- file.path(output_path, paste0("topic_labels_K", chosen_K, ".csv"))
write_csv(label_export, labels_file)
cat("Labels saved to:", labels_file, "\n")

8c. Manual Label Overrides (Optional)

Review the auto-generated labels above. Override any that need better names. Only uncomment and edit the lines you want to change.

# ============================================================
# MANUAL OVERRIDES - uncomment and edit as needed
# ============================================================
topic_labels[1] <- "Historical People and Places"
topic_labels[2] <- "National and International Women's Councils"
topic_labels[3] <- "Fictional Stories"
#topic_labels[4] <- "Fictional Stories"
topic_labels[5] <- "Relief Society and Conference Reports"
topic_labels[6] <- "Poetic Prose and Literature"
topic_labels[7] <- "Poems"
topic_labels[8] <- "Relief Society Board Meeting Reports"
topic_labels[9] <- "Resolutions of Respect"
topic_labels[10] <- "Primary Association Reports"
topic_labels[11] <- "Railroad Advertisements"
#topic_labels[12] <- "Sericulture"
topic_labels[13] <- "Birthday Announcements"
topic_labels[14] <- "International News and Histories"
topic_labels[15] <- "Motherhood"
topic_labels[16] <- "Political Party News and Events"
topic_labels[17] <- "Moral Stories"
topic_labels[18] <- "Community Celebrations"
topic_labels[19] <- "Obituaries"
topic_labels[20] <- "Pioneer Legacy"
topic_labels[21] <- "Editorial Advice"
topic_labels[22] <- "Sericulture"
topic_labels[23] <- "Advertisements"
topic_labels[24] <- "Women's Education and Refinement"
#topic_labels[25] <- "Utah Woman's Press Club Reports"
topic_labels[26] <- "Mourning and Memoriam"
topic_labels[27] <- "Household Hints and Recipes"
topic_labels[28] <- "Textile Advertisements"
topic_labels[29] <- "Current Events and News"
topic_labels[31] <- "L.D.S. Women of the Past"
topic_labels[32] <- "Polygamy and Political News"
topic_labels[33] <- "Polygamy and Political News"
topic_labels[34] <- "Gospel Doctrine and Spiritual Kingdom"
topic_labels[35] <- "Education and Technical Training"




cat("FINAL TOPIC LABELS\n")
cat(rep("=", 70), "\n\n", sep = "")
for (i in 1:chosen_K) {
  cat("  Topic", i, ":", topic_labels[i], "\n")
}

8c.1 Checkpoint — Save Inputs for Advanced Analysis

Writes the minimal, theta-aligned objects that WEadvancedtextanalysis.Rmd (and the Python compute scripts) consume. Runs BEFORE the heavy visualization chunks, so you can produce the K-specific handoff by knitting/running only up to this point — no need to survive the timeout-prone plotting section.

Outputs (in output_path, suffixed by K so each model is self-describing):

  • advanced_input_K{K}.rds — R list for the advanced notebook (stm_model, meta, topic_labels, modeled_raw, modeled_clean).
  • advanced_docs_K{K}.csv — one row per modeled document, row-aligned to theta, with the ORIGINAL article text for transformer embeddings.
  • advanced_theta_K{K}.csv — D×K document-topic matrix, same row order.
  • advanced_topics_K{K}.csv — topic number, label, top FREX/PROB words.
# --- Align original + cleaned text to the modeled documents (theta rows) ---
# meta is row-aligned to stm_model$theta by construction (out$meta from
# prepDocuments). We map each modeled doc back to processed_texts by doc_id.
modeled_idx   <- match(meta$doc_id, processed_texts$doc_id)
modeled_raw   <- processed_texts$text[modeled_idx]        # natural-language text
modeled_clean <- processed_texts$text_final[modeled_idx]  # cleaned bag-of-words

stopifnot(
  nrow(meta) == nrow(stm_model$theta),
  length(modeled_raw) == nrow(meta),
  !anyNA(modeled_idx)
)

# --- Doc-level table for Python (row order == theta row order) ---
adv_docs <- tibble::tibble(
  row_index    = seq_len(nrow(meta)),   # 1..D, the theta row this doc occupies
  doc_id       = meta$doc_id,
  filename     = meta$filename,
  title        = meta$Title,
  author       = meta$Author,
  article_type = meta$Article_Type,
  volume_num   = meta$volume_num,
  year         = meta$year,
  issue        = meta$issue,
  n_words      = stringr::str_count(modeled_raw, "\\S+"),
  text         = modeled_raw            # ORIGINAL text for transformer models
)

# --- Theta matrix as a data frame (row-aligned), labeled columns ---
theta_df <- as.data.frame(stm_model$theta)
colnames(theta_df) <- paste0("Topic", seq_len(ncol(theta_df)))
theta_df <- dplyr::bind_cols(row_index = seq_len(nrow(theta_df)), theta_df)

# --- Topic word table ---
.tw <- labelTopics(stm_model, n = 12)
topics_df <- tibble::tibble(
  topic_num = seq_len(chosen_K),
  label     = topic_labels,
  frex      = apply(.tw$frex, 1, paste, collapse = ", "),
  prob      = apply(.tw$prob, 1, paste, collapse = ", ")
)

# --- Write everything, suffixed by K ---
ksfx <- paste0("_K", chosen_K)
saveRDS(
  list(stm_model = stm_model, meta = meta, topic_labels = topic_labels,
       modeled_raw = modeled_raw, modeled_clean = modeled_clean,
       fit_date = Sys.time()),
  file.path(output_path, paste0("advanced_input", ksfx, ".rds"))
)
readr::write_csv(adv_docs,  file.path(output_path, paste0("advanced_docs",   ksfx, ".csv")))
readr::write_csv(theta_df,  file.path(output_path, paste0("advanced_theta",  ksfx, ".csv")))
readr::write_csv(topics_df, file.path(output_path, paste0("advanced_topics", ksfx, ".csv")))

cat("Advanced-analysis handoff saved for K =", chosen_K, "\n")
cat("  Documents:", nrow(adv_docs), " | Topics:", chosen_K,
    " | Year range:", paste(range(adv_docs$year, na.rm = TRUE), collapse = "-"), "\n")
cat("  Files in:", output_path, "\n")

8d. Find Examples for a Specific Topic

Change topic_to_examine to preview example articles for any topic before finalizing labels.

# ============================================================
# CHANGE THIS to examine any topic
# ===========================================================
topic_to_examine <- 8

n_examples <- 15

cat(rep("=", 70), "\n", sep = "")
cat("TOPIC", topic_to_examine, ":", topic_labels[topic_to_examine], "\n")
cat(rep("=", 70), "\n", sep = "")
cat("FREX:", paste(all_top_words$frex[topic_to_examine, 1:10], collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat("PROB:", paste(all_top_words$prob[topic_to_examine, 1:10], collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat(rep("-", 70), "\n\n", sep = "")

# modeled_texts was stored after prepDocuments — guaranteed to match model docs
thoughts <- findThoughts(stm_model,
                         texts = modeled_texts,
                         topics = topic_to_examine,
                         n = n_examples)

thought_indices <- thoughts$index[[1]]

for (i in seq_along(thought_indices)) {
  idx <- thought_indices[i]
  row <- processed_texts[idx, ]

  cat(sprintf("Example %d of %d:\n", i, length(thought_indices)))
  cat(sprintf("  File:   %s\n", row$filename))
  cat(sprintf("  Title:  %s\n", ifelse(is.na(row$Title), "(unknown)", row$Title)))
  cat(sprintf("  Author: %s\n", ifelse(is.na(row$Author) | row$Author == "Unknown", "-", row$Author)))
  cat(sprintf("  Type:   %s\n", ifelse(is.na(row$Article_Type), "-", row$Article_Type)))
  cat(sprintf("  Vol %s, %s, Issue %s\n\n", row$volume_num, row$year, row$issue))
  cat(substr(thoughts$docs[[1]][i], 1, 400), "\n\n")
  cat(rep("-", 50), "\n\n", sep = "")
}

9. Link Topics to Articles

This is the key section: for each topic, find the articles with the highest proportion of that topic. These are your evidentiary examples.

# Get document-topic proportions (make.dt merges meta columns automatically)
doc_topics <- make.dt(stm_model, meta = meta)

# Check what columns are available
cat("Columns in doc_topics:\n")
cat(paste(names(doc_topics), collapse = ", "), "\n\n")

# Build the full linked table
# make.dt() already includes all meta columns, so no join needed
topic_article_link <- doc_topics %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("Topic"),
    names_to = "topic",
    values_to = "proportion"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    topic_num = as.integer(str_remove(topic, "Topic")),
    topic_label = topic_labels[topic_num]
  ) %>%
  select(topic_num, topic_label, proportion,
         any_of(c("filename", "Title", "Author", "Article_Type",
                  "volume_num", "year", "issue"))) %>%
  arrange(topic_num, desc(proportion))

cat("Linked table:", nrow(topic_article_link), "rows\n")
cat("(", nrow(doc_topics), "documents x", chosen_K, "topics )\n")

9b. Dominant Topic Assignment

Each article is tagged with its single strongest topic, plus a confidence flag based on whether the proportion exceeds a threshold.

# ============================================================
# THRESHOLD: articles above this % are "strong" exemplars
# ============================================================
exemplar_threshold <- 0.40  # 40%

# Assign dominant topic to each document
# (doc_topics already has meta columns from make.dt)
dominant_topics <- doc_topics %>%
  pivot_longer(starts_with("Topic"), names_to = "topic", values_to = "proportion") %>%
  mutate(topic_num = as.integer(str_remove(topic, "Topic"))) %>%
  group_by(doc_id) %>%
  slice_max(proportion, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    topic_label = topic_labels[topic_num],
    strong_exemplar = proportion >= exemplar_threshold,
    proportion_pct = round(proportion * 100, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(doc_id, any_of(c("filename", "Title", "Author", "Article_Type",
         "volume_num", "year", "issue")),
         dominant_topic = topic_num, topic_label, proportion_pct, strong_exemplar) %>%
  arrange(dominant_topic, desc(proportion_pct))

cat("Dominant topic assigned to", nrow(dominant_topics), "documents\n")
cat("Strong exemplars (>", exemplar_threshold * 100, "%):", sum(dominant_topics$strong_exemplar), "\n\n")

# Summary of articles per topic
dominant_topics %>%
  group_by(dominant_topic, topic_label) %>%
  summarise(
    n_articles = n(),
    n_strong = sum(strong_exemplar),
    avg_proportion = round(mean(proportion_pct), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  print(n = chosen_K)
# Show strong exemplars per topic
cat("\nSTRONG EXEMPLARS (>", exemplar_threshold * 100, "% topic proportion):\n")

for (t in 1:chosen_K) {
  strong <- dominant_topics %>%
    filter(dominant_topic == t, strong_exemplar)

  cat("\n", rep("-", 60), "\n", sep = "")
  cat("Topic", t, ":", topic_labels[t], " (", nrow(strong), " strong exemplars)\n")

  if (nrow(strong) > 0) {
    for (i in 1:min(10, nrow(strong))) {
      row <- strong[i, ]
      cat(sprintf("  [%s%%] %s | %s | Vol %s %s\n",
                  row$proportion_pct,
                  ifelse(is.na(row$Title), row$filename, row$Title),
                  ifelse(is.na(row$Author) | row$Author == "Unknown", "-", row$Author),
                  row$volume_num, row$year))
    }
  } else {
    cat("  (none above threshold - try lowering exemplar_threshold)\n")
  }
}

10. Top Articles per Topic

For each topic bucket, here are the top 15 articles ranked by how strongly they express that topic. Use these as evidentiary examples.

top_n_per_topic <- 3

for (t in 1:chosen_K) {
  cat("\n", rep("=", 70), "\n", sep = "")
  cat("TOPIC", t, ":", topic_labels[t], "\n")
  cat(rep("=", 70), "\n")
  cat("FREX:", paste(all_top_words$frex[t, 1:10], collapse = ", "), "\n\n")

  top_arts <- topic_article_link %>%
    filter(topic_num == t) %>%
    head(top_n_per_topic)

  for (i in 1:nrow(top_arts)) {
    row <- top_arts[i, ]
    cat(sprintf("  %2d. [%.1f%%] Vol %s, %s, Issue %s | %s | %s | %s\n",
                i,
                row$proportion * 100,
                row$volume_num,
                row$year,
                row$issue,
                ifelse(is.na(row$Title), row$filename, row$Title),
                ifelse(is.na(row$Author) | row$Author == "Unknown", "-", row$Author),
                ifelse(is.na(row$Article_Type), "-", row$Article_Type)))
  }
}

11. Interactive Article Browser

Use this searchable table to find articles by topic, type, author, etc.

# Top 25 articles per topic for the interactive table
browse_data <- topic_article_link %>%
  group_by(topic_num) %>%
  slice_max(proportion, n = 25) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    proportion_pct = round(proportion * 100, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Topic = topic_num,
    Label = topic_label,
    `%` = proportion_pct,
    Title,
    Author,
    Type = Article_Type,
    Vol = volume_num,
    Year = year,
    Issue = issue,
    Filename = filename
  )

datatable(
  browse_data,
  filter = "top",
  options = list(pageLength = 20, scrollX = TRUE),
  caption = "Top 25 articles per topic - filter by topic, type, author, etc."
)

12. Topic Prevalence Summary

topic_props <- colMeans(doc_topics %>% select(starts_with("Topic")))

prevalence_df <- tibble(
  topic_num = 1:chosen_K,
  label = topic_labels,
  prevalence = topic_props,
  pct = round(topic_props * 100, 1)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(prevalence))

# Bar plot
par(mar = c(5, 22, 4, 2))
bar_y <- barplot(
  height    = prevalence_df$prevalence,
  names.arg = prevalence_df$label,
  horiz     = TRUE,
  las       = 1,
  col       = "#CD661D",
  border    = "#8B4513",
  main      = paste0("Topic Prevalence - Woman's Exponent (Vols ",
                     min(input_vol_nums), "-", max(input_vol_nums), ")"),
  xlab      = "Expected Topic Proportion",
  cex.names = 0.8,
  xlim      = c(0, max(prevalence_df$prevalence) * 1.3)
)
text(
  x      = prevalence_df$prevalence + 0.005,
  y      = bar_y,                              # ← was seq(0.7, by = 1.2, ...)
  labels = paste0(prevalence_df$pct, "%"),
  cex    = 0.7, col = "#8B4513", font = 2
)
par(mar = c(5, 4, 4, 2))

print(prevalence_df %>% select(topic_num, label, pct), n = Inf)
write_csv(
  prevalence_df %>% select(topic_num, label, prevalence, pct),
  file.path(output_path, paste0("topic_prevalence_K", chosen_K, ".csv"))
)

13. STM Summary Plot

plot(stm_model,
     type = "summary",
     n = min(10, chosen_K),
     main = paste("Topic Prevalence (K =", chosen_K, ")"),
     xlim = c(0, 0.3))

14. Lollipop Prevalence with Top Words

library(ggplot2)

top_words_display <- labelTopics(stm_model, n = 10)

labels_df <- tibble(
  topic = 1:chosen_K,
  name = topic_labels,
  words = sapply(1:chosen_K, function(i) paste(top_words_display$prob[i, 1:10], collapse = ", ")),
  full_label = paste0(name, ": ", words),
  prevalence = topic_props
)

ggplot(labels_df, aes(x = prevalence, y = reorder(full_label, prevalence))) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = prevalence, y = full_label, yend = full_label),
               linewidth = 1.2, color = "black") +
  geom_point(aes(x = prevalence, y = full_label), size = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = paste("Topic Prevalence (K =", chosen_K, ")"),
    x = "Expected Topic Proportions",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.35), breaks = seq(0, 0.35, 0.05))

15. Topic Prevalence Over Time

cat("Estimating time effects...\n")

prep_time <- estimateEffect(
  formula = 1:chosen_K ~ s(year),
  stmobj = stm_model,
  metadata = meta,
  uncertainty = "Global"
)

cat("Estimation complete.\n\n")

# Get top 6 most prevalent topics
top_6_topics <- order(topic_props, decreasing = TRUE)[1:min(6, chosen_K)]

par(mfrow = c(2, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))
for (topic_num in top_6_topics) {
  plot(
    prep_time,
    covariate = "year",
    model = stm_model,
    topics = topic_num,
    method = "continuous",
    xlim = range(meta$year, na.rm = TRUE),
    main = str_wrap(topic_labels[topic_num], 30),
    xlab = "Year",
    ylab = "Topic Proportion",
    linecol = "#3b82f6",
    printlegend = TRUE,
    ci.level = 0.95,
    lwd = 2
  )
}
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

16. Scatter Plot - Top 6 Topics Over Time

topic_time_data <- make.dt(stm_model, meta = meta) %>%
  select(starts_with("Topic"), year) %>%
  filter(!is.na(year)) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarize(across(starts_with("Topic"), mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_longer(starts_with("Topic"), names_to = "topic", values_to = "prevalence") %>%
  mutate(
    topic_num = as.numeric(str_remove(topic, "Topic")),
    topic_label = str_wrap(topic_labels[topic_num], 30)
  )

topic_time_top6 <- topic_time_data %>%
  filter(topic_num %in% top_6_topics)

ggplot(topic_time_top6, aes(x = year, y = prevalence)) +
  geom_line(color = "#E0EEEE", size = 1.5) +
  geom_point(color = "#E0EEEE", size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#CD661D", linetype = "dashed",
              se = TRUE, alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~topic_label, ncol = 3, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Topic Prevalence Over Time: Top 6 Most Prevalent Topics",
    subtitle = "Points = actual data | Dashed line = trend",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean Topic Proportion"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "grey40")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.1))

17. All Topics Over Time

ggplot(topic_time_data, aes(x = year, y = prevalence)) +
  geom_line(color = "#CD661d", size = 0.8) +
  geom_point(color = "#CD661d", size = 1.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~topic_label, ncol = 4, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Complete Topic Evolution Over Time (Woman's Exponent)"),
    subtitle = paste0("All ", chosen_K, " topics across years"),
    x = "Year",
    y = "Topic Proportion"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = 7, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.1))

18. Single Topic Detail

# CHANGE THIS to examine any topic in detail
topic_to_examine <- 1

topic_detail <- topic_time_data %>%
  filter(topic_num == topic_to_examine)

ggplot(topic_detail, aes(x = year, y = prevalence)) +
  geom_line(color = "#3b82f6", size = 1.5) +
  geom_point(color = "#3b82f6", size = 4, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#ef4444",
              fill = "#fecaca", alpha = 0.3, size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = topic_labels[topic_to_examine],
    subtitle = "Points = annual averages | Red band = smoothed trend with confidence interval",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Topic Proportion"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "#64748b"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(topic_detail$year),
                                   max(topic_detail$year), by = 2))

19. Topic Correlation Network

mod.out.corr <- topicCorr(stm_model)
plot(mod.out.corr, main = "Topic Correlation Network")

20. Word Clouds

library(wordcloud)

cloud(stm_model,
      topic = 1,
      scale = c(3, 0.5),
      main = topic_labels[1])
# Set eval=TRUE to generate word clouds for all topics
for (i in 1:chosen_K) {
  cloud(stm_model,
        topic = i,
        scale = c(3, 0.5),
        main = topic_labels[i])
}

21. Topic Quality

topicQuality(stm_model, documents = docs)

22. Compare Two Topics

# Change the topic numbers to compare different topics
plot(stm_model,
     type = "perspectives",
     topics = c(1, 2),
     main = paste("Comparing", topic_labels[1], "vs", topic_labels[2]))

23. Trend Summary Statistics

topic_trends <- topic_time_data %>%
  group_by(topic_num, topic_label) %>%
  summarise(
    start_prev = first(prevalence),
    end_prev   = last(prevalence),
    change     = end_prev - start_prev,
    pct_change = round((change / start_prev) * 100, 1),
    trend      = case_when(
      change >  0.005 ~ "Increasing",
      change < -0.005 ~ "Decreasing",
      TRUE            ~ "Stable"
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(pct_change)))

cat("TOPIC CHANGE SUMMARY\n")
cat(rep("=", 50), "\n\n", sep = "")

for (i in 1:nrow(topic_trends)) {
  direction <- ifelse(topic_trends$pct_change[i] > 0, "increased", "decreased")
  cat(sprintf("Topic %d (%s): %s by %.1f%% over the study period\n",
              topic_trends$topic_num[i],
              topic_trends$topic_label[i],
              direction,
              abs(topic_trends$pct_change[i])))
}

cat("\n\nSUMMARY TABLE\n")
print(topic_trends %>% select(topic_num, topic_label, pct_change, trend))

23b. Detailed Topic Change Over Time

Comprehensive table showing each topic’s percentage change from the earliest year to the latest year, plus year-over-year percentage changes to see when each topic rose or fell.

cat(rep("=", 70), "\n", sep = "")
cat("DETAILED TOPIC PERCENTAGE CHANGE OVER TIME\n")
cat(rep("=", 70), "\n\n", sep = "")

# ============================================================
# Part 1: Overall percentage change (first year -> last year)
# ============================================================
overall_change <- topic_time_data %>%
  group_by(topic_num, topic_label) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  summarise(
    first_year       = first(year),
    last_year        = last(year),
    first_prevalence = first(prevalence),
    last_prevalence  = last(prevalence),
    abs_change_pp    = (last_prevalence - first_prevalence) * 100,   # pp
    pct_change       = ifelse(first_prevalence > 0.001,
                              (last_prevalence - first_prevalence) / first_prevalence * 100,
                              NA_real_),
    mean_prevalence  = mean(prevalence),
    max_prevalence   = max(prevalence),
    min_prevalence   = min(prevalence),
    peak_year        = year[which.max(prevalence)],
    trough_year      = year[which.min(prevalence)],
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    direction = case_when(
      pct_change >  10 ~ "Growing",
      pct_change < -10 ~ "Declining",
      TRUE             ~ "Stable"
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(pct_change))

cat(sprintf("OVERALL CHANGE (%d -> %d):\n\n",
            min(topic_time_data$year), max(topic_time_data$year)))
cat(sprintf("%-40s  %8s  %8s  %10s  %9s  %10s\n",
            "Topic", "First %", "Last %", "Change pp", "% Change", "Direction"))
cat(rep("-", 95), "\n", sep = "")
for (i in 1:nrow(overall_change)) {
  r <- overall_change[i, ]
  pct_str <- ifelse(is.na(r$pct_change), "N/A", sprintf("%+.1f%%", r$pct_change))
  cat(sprintf("%-40s  %7.2f%%  %7.2f%%  %+8.2fpp  %9s  %10s\n",
              str_trunc(paste0("T", r$topic_num, ": ", r$topic_label), 40),
              r$first_prevalence * 100, r$last_prevalence * 100,
              r$abs_change_pp, pct_str, r$direction))
}

cat("\n\nPEAK / TROUGH YEARS:\n\n")
cat(sprintf("%-40s  %10s  %10s  %10s\n",
            "Topic", "Peak Year", "Peak %", "Trough Year"))
cat(rep("-", 80), "\n", sep = "")
for (i in 1:nrow(overall_change)) {
  r <- overall_change[i, ]
  cat(sprintf("%-40s  %10d  %9.2f%%  %10d\n",
              str_trunc(paste0("T", r$topic_num, ": ", r$topic_label), 40),
              r$peak_year, r$max_prevalence * 100, r$trough_year))
}

# ============================================================
# Part 2: Year-over-year percentage changes (wide table)
# ============================================================
cat("\n\nYEAR-OVER-YEAR PERCENTAGE CHANGE (%):\n")
cat("(positive = prevalence grew that year; negative = prevalence dropped)\n\n")

yoy_change <- topic_time_data %>%
  arrange(topic_num, year) %>%
  group_by(topic_num) %>%
  mutate(
    prev_prevalence = lag(prevalence),
    yoy_pct = ifelse(!is.na(prev_prevalence) & prev_prevalence > 0.001,
                     (prevalence - prev_prevalence) / prev_prevalence * 100,
                     NA_real_)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(topic_num, topic_label, year, yoy_pct)

yoy_wide <- yoy_change %>%
  select(topic_num, topic_label, year, yoy_pct) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = yoy_pct) %>%
  arrange(topic_num) %>%
  mutate(topic = paste0("T", topic_num, ": ", str_trunc(topic_label, 30))) %>%
  select(topic, everything(), -topic_num, -topic_label)

print(yoy_wide, n = chosen_K, width = Inf)

# ============================================================
# Part 3: Export detailed change table to CSV
# ============================================================
overall_change_export <- overall_change %>%
  mutate(
    first_prevalence_pct = round(first_prevalence * 100, 2),
    last_prevalence_pct  = round(last_prevalence * 100, 2),
    mean_prevalence_pct  = round(mean_prevalence * 100, 2),
    max_prevalence_pct   = round(max_prevalence * 100, 2),
    min_prevalence_pct   = round(min_prevalence * 100, 2),
    abs_change_pp        = round(abs_change_pp, 2),
    pct_change           = round(pct_change, 1)
  ) %>%
  select(topic_num, topic_label, first_year, last_year,
         first_prevalence_pct, last_prevalence_pct,
         abs_change_pp, pct_change, direction,
         mean_prevalence_pct, max_prevalence_pct, peak_year,
